skill.name: fullstack-dev
skill.description: Guides full-stack application architecture, backend service patterns, API integration, authentication, configuration, testing, and production hardening.
skill.summary: Build full-stack apps with structured backend, API, auth, testing, and deployment patterns.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high patterns, but review shows most are Markdown examples for full-stack development, not executable marketplace code. No prompt injection or malicious data exfiltration intent was found. One medium-risk instructional issue remains: the upload guidance builds an object key from a raw filename query parameter.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe File Upload Key Construction in Example Guidance
medium_findings.description: The presigned upload example reads filename from the query string and interpolates it into an object key. This can teach unsafe handling of untrusted filenames unless callers sanitize path separators, normalize names, and enforce extension and content-type policy.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is documentation, not executable marketplace logic, which limits immediate impact. The pattern is still semantically risky because it uses a user-controlled filename in a storage key without validation.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings occur in fenced Markdown examples and verification checklists, such as build commands and curl smoke tests. They do not create autonomous command execution by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations are static Markdown guidance and shell snippets for user-run project verification. No hidden script, hook, or automatic execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Network and Environment Access Are Expected Full-Stack Patterns
low_findings.description: Network calls and environment variable reads are shown as application examples for API clients, CORS, SSR auth, and test containers. The reviewed examples use configuration values for local app behavior rather than sending secrets to an unrelated endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are common for full-stack scaffolding and are presented as illustrative snippets. Review found no evidence that credentials are exfiltrated or that the skill performs network activity itself.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Sensitive Storage Alerts Are Mostly Protective Guidance
low_findings.description: Several high static alerts come from security recommendations that warn users not to use weak hashing or browser localStorage for tokens. These are protective statements, not unsafe implementation instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly recommend bcrypt or argon2 and warn against risky token storage. The static analyzer appears to key on security terms without interpreting the surrounding negative guidance.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM Database Alert Is a Substring False Positive
low_findings.description: The critical SAM database alert points to PostgreSQL row-level security DDL, not Windows credential database access. No evidence found for Windows SAM access or credential dumping instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context is SQL enabling row-level security for tenant isolation. It contains no Windows path, registry hive, or SAM credential access behavior.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The scanner combined code snippets for commands, network access, filesystem-style paths, and credentials into a critical heuristic. Context review shows these are distributed educational examples for app development, not a coordinated exfiltration workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky primitives exist in documentation for normal application construction, but they are not connected into an executable data collection or exfiltration path. A prompt-injection phrase search also found no suspicious override language.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe File Upload Key Construction in Example Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The presigned upload example reads filename from the query string and interpolates it into an object key. This can teach unsafe handling of untrusted filenames unless callers sanitize path separators, normalize names, and enforce extension and content-type policy.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code is documentation, not executable marketplace logic, which limits immediate impact. The pattern is still semantically risky because it uses a user-controlled filename in a storage key without validation.

content.value_statement: Full-stack projects fail when backend structure, API contracts, auth, and deployment checks are improvised. This skill provides practical architecture patterns and checklists for building maintainable apps with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Guides feature-first backend structure with controllers, services, repositories, and typed errors.
Provides checklists for configuration, authentication, database access, CORS, logging, and health checks.
Shows frontend-backend integration patterns for typed fetch, React Query, tRPC, and generated clients.
Covers file uploads, SSE, WebSocket, background jobs, caching, and production hardening.
Includes focused references for API design, database schema, testing, Django, auth flow, and releases.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not a complete project generator by itself.
Examples must be adapted to the selected framework, versions, and hosting platform.
Some code snippets are illustrative and need security review before production use.
It is not intended for pure frontend styling or isolated database schema work.

use_cases.title: Scaffold a New Product API
use_cases.description: Create a backend structure, health checks, configuration, validation, and API client plan for a new app.

use_cases.title: Standardize App Architecture
use_cases.description: Review an existing service and align controllers, services, repositories, errors, logging, and configuration.

use_cases.title: Prepare for Production Release
use_cases.description: Use testing, environment, auth, CORS, and release checklists before deploying a full-stack feature.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic CRUD App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a small CRUD app with a REST API, database, auth, and frontend API client. State the architecture decisions before code.

prompt_templates.title: Add Auth to an Existing App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add login, refresh tokens, protected routes, CORS, and frontend request handling to my existing full-stack app.

prompt_templates.title: Review Backend Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my backend module boundaries, service layer, repository layer, error handling, logging, and environment configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production-Ready Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a production-ready service with typed APIs, test strategy, observability, caching, background jobs, release gates, and rollback notes.

output_examples.input: Design a todo app with React and Express.
output_examples.output: A decision summary, feature-first folder plan, endpoint list, validation approach, auth choice, API client plan, and verification checklist.

output_examples.input: Review this backend for production readiness.
Add real-time updates to this dashboard.
output_examples.output: A recommendation between polling, SSE, and WebSocket, with backend responsibilities, frontend state handling, and smoke-test steps.

best_practices: State stack, database, API style, auth strategy, and real-time needs before implementation.
Validate configuration, input, errors, and authentication at clear boundaries.
Run build, smoke, integration, and release checks before handoff.
anti_patterns: Using the examples without adapting security controls to the project context.
Mixing business logic directly into routes or controllers.
Skipping verification after scaffolding backend and frontend integration.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete app automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides architecture, patterns, and checklists. The agent still needs your stack, requirements, and repository context.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it only for Node.js projects?
faq.answer: No. It includes Node.js, Python, Go, Django, REST, GraphQL, tRPC, and related full-stack patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers JWT, sessions, refresh tokens, middleware order, RBAC, and frontend token handling.

faq.question: Can it help with production hardening?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for CORS, secrets, logging, health checks, testing, release gates, and rollback planning.

faq.question: What should users review carefully?
faq.answer: Review illustrative snippets for security details, especially file upload handling, secret management, and environment-specific URLs.
